subsystem: UPX (Ultimate Packer for eXecutables) ELF64 self-decompression stub host code: PackLinuxElf64::unpack(). This is the routine that reverses UPX packing of a 64-bit ELF file — it parses the UPX overlay headers (l_info, p_info, b_info), decompresses the saved original ELF Ehdr/Phdrs into a scratch buffer, then walks the program headers to reconstruct and write each PT_LOAD extent (and, for shared libraries, restores PT_DYNAMIC/DT_INIT) to the OutputFile.
provenance: Identified by the UPX-specific symbol vocabulary: the class/method PackLinuxElf64::unpack, UPX types and helpers upx_uint64_t / upx_byte / b_info / p_info / l_info / linfo / ph (PackHeader), the byte-swap accessors get_te16/get_te32/get_te64/get_le32, upx_adler32, UPX_MAGIC_LE32, unpackExtent/decompress/find_LOAD_gap/mem_size_valid/up4/funpad4, and the UPX-style throw* error helpers. The banner 'FILE: corpus/train/train_0138_cwe476.cpp' and the header comment '/* sample: primevul_train_2349_fixed */' confirm the origin (a PrimeVul C/C++ sample). Elf64_Ehdr/Elf64_Phdr/Elf64_Dyn are standard ELF wire structures.
trust_boundary: The entire input file 'fi' is attacker-controlled: it is the untrusted UPX-packed ELF that the user asks upx to unpack. Every field read from it through get_te16/te32/te64/le32 is hostile — including e_phnum (both ehdri.e_phnum and the decompressed ehdr->e_phnum), e_entry, e_machine, e_ident, the p_info fields p_filesize/p_blocksize, the b_info fields sz_unc/sz_cpr, linfo.l_lsize, and every Elf64_Phdr field (p_type, p_offset, p_filesz, p_vaddr, p_flags) and Elf64_Dyn field decoded from the decompressed header block and from ibuf. The output file 'fo' is trusted (and may legitimately be NULL in a test/list pass, which is why every write is guarded by 'if (fo)'). The scratch destinations — the 1024-byte stack union u.buf and the heap buffer ibuf sized blocksize+OVERHEAD — are the assets an over-read/over-write corrupts.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point of the ELF64 unpacker. 'fo' is the output file to receive the reconstructed original executable (it can be NULL for a dry run). The first gate insists that the program-header table begins immediately after the ELF header (e_phoff == sizeof(Elf64_Ehdr)); if not, unpacking aborts. e_phoff comes from the packed file's own ehdr (a member already parsed earlier), so this is a coarse structural sanity check, not a full validation of the phdr table.

c_phnum is the number of program headers in the *compressed* container's ELF header (ehdri), read as a target-endian 16-bit value so it can be 0..65535, fully attacker-controlled. old_data_off/old_data_len will later hold the file offset and size of the second PT_LOAD of a shared library, and old_dtinit the saved DT_INIT value to restore. All three are 64-bit unsigned quantities that will be filled from attacker-supplied phdr fields.

Chooses the on-disk size of the per-block b_info header. Normally it is sizeof(b_info); but for very old EM_386 packs whose entry point is below 0x401180 it is shrunk to two unsigned words (8 bytes). e_entry and e_machine are read from the untrusted ehdri, so the attacker selects which b_info layout the parser uses; szb_info is threaded through every subsequent readx and seek that skips a block header.

Positions the input just before the UPX overlay and reads the l_info structure. lsize is taken from linfo.l_lsize as a target-endian 16-bit field (0..65535) — it is the size of the UPX loader stub, and it is attacker-controlled. 'overlay_offset' is a class member computed earlier from the packed file; the subtraction 'overlay_offset - sizeof(l_info)' is a file position, not a memory index.

Reads the UPX p_info record and extracts orig_file_size (the size the original executable should decompress to) and blocksize (the maximum size of any single compressed block). Both are 32-bit target-endian, attacker-controlled. The guard rejects the file if the packed file is larger than the claimed original, if blocksize exceeds orig_file_size, or if mem_size_valid(1, blocksize, OVERHEAD) reports that blocksize+OVERHEAD would be an unsafe allocation. This is the key bound that later sizes the heap buffer ibuf, so the check matters a great deal.

Declares the fixed 1024-byte scratch buffer u.buf that will receive the decompressed original ELF header plus its program-header table. 'ehdr' aliases the start of u.buf as an Elf64_Ehdr; 'phdr' is a program-header cursor initialised to NULL. Because sizeof(Elf64_Ehdr) is 64 and sizeof(Elf64_Phdr) is 56, at most (1024-64)/56 ≈ 17 program headers physically fit in u.buf — a hard capacity that later loops driven by the attacker-chosen e_phnum do not enforce.

Allocates the heap decompression buffer ibuf with capacity blocksize+OVERHEAD (blocksize was bounded above), then reads the first b_info block header. sz_unc/sz_cpr are the uncompressed and compressed lengths of the first block, both attacker-controlled 32-bit values, stored into ph.u_len/ph.c_len. The guard rejects zero lengths, a compressed length larger than the whole file, and — crucially — an uncompressed length larger than sizeof(u) (1024), which bounds the decompression destination for the header block. filter_cto stores the unfilter parameter byte.

Re-checks that the compressed length fits the heap buffer and the uncompressed length fits u.buf, reads ph.c_len compressed bytes into ibuf, and decompresses them into u.buf via 'ehdr'. After this call, u.buf holds the reconstructed original Elf64_Ehdr followed by its program headers. The destination bound (sizeof(u) >= ph.u_len) protects the raw decompression byte count, but not the *number of program headers* that later loops will read out of u.buf.

Cross-checks the decompressed header against the container header (type/machine/version/flags/ehsize and the first EI_OSABI bytes of e_ident) to ensure the reconstructed header is self-consistent. Note both operands (ehdr->* and ehdri.*) are attacker-influenced, so this is an internal-consistency test rather than a trust anchor. Finally it rewinds the input by szb_info+ph.c_len so the block just consumed can be re-read by unpackExtent.

u_phnum is the program-header count taken from the *decompressed* original ehdr (ehdr->e_phnum), a target-endian 16-bit value the attacker controls (0..65535). It drives every phdr-walk loop below over both u.buf and ibuf. total_in/total_out accumulate consumed/produced byte counts, and c_adler/u_adler seed the running Adler-32 checksums verified at the end.

Determines whether this is a shared library by searching the container's program headers (phdri/c_phnum) for a PT_DYNAMIC entry. For a shlib it first unpacks the header extent, then re-reads the whole prefix of the file into ibuf using length 'overlay_offset + sizeof(hbuf) + szb_info + ph.c_len', adjusts overlay_offset, and (if writing) emits the uncompressed prefix via fo->write(ibuf + ph.u_len, overlay_offset - ph.u_len). Both the readx length and the write length are computed from attacker-controlled quantities (overlay_offset, ph.c_len, ph.u_len) that are unrelated to ibuf's blocksize+OVERHEAD capacity.

Two phdr walks, both bounded only by the attacker-chosen u_phnum. The first walks the program headers embedded in ibuf (just past the ELF header) to find the second PT_LOAD and capture its file offset/size into old_data_off/old_data_len. The second walks the phdrs in u.buf to find the first PT_LOAD and sets ph.u_len to that segment's p_filesz minus overlay_offset — an unsigned 64-bit subtraction — then unpacks that many bytes. The subtraction assumes p_filesz >= overlay_offset.

The main-executable path: iterate u_phnum program headers in u.buf, and for each PT_LOAD extract filesz and offset (both narrowed from 64-bit to 'unsigned', truncating the high 32 bits), seek the output there, and unpack that extent — the first executable (PF_X) segment gets the unfilter flag. filesz flows directly into unpackExtent as the amount to produce; nothing here re-checks it against orig_file_size, so the per-segment sizes are trusted from the decompressed header.

Resets the cursor to the container's program-header array phdri and scans c_phnum entries for the first PT_LOAD to recover its virtual base load_va. phdri is a class member holding the parsed container phdrs; the loop dereferences it without a local NULL check, and c_phnum is attacker-controlled.

If the loader stub is not at the end of the file (or always for a shlib), skip past it: align, read a 6-word d_info array, and if no DT_INIT was captured yet, take old_dtinit from d_info[2] or d_info[3] depending on whether d_info[0] is zero. Then advance by lsize - sizeof(d_info). Because lsize (from linfo.l_lsize) is attacker-controlled and only 16 bits, the seek delta 'lsize - sizeof(d_info)' is computed in unsigned/size_t arithmetic and wraps huge if lsize < 24 — a file-position anomaly rather than a memory-safety bug.

Handles the padding gaps between loadable segments. It re-bases the phdr cursor at u.buf+sizeof(Ehdr) and, over two u_phnum-bounded loops, first computes hi_offset (the maximum p_offset) then, for each gap reported by find_LOAD_gap, seeks the output to p_offset+p_filesz and unpacks the gap. Every access phdr[j] indexes the fixed 1024-byte u.buf using the attacker-controlled u_phnum with no capacity check.

Reads a trailing b_info to confirm end-of-stream: a zero uncompressed size marks EOF, in which case the compressed-size slot must equal the UPX magic (UPX_MAGIC_LE32); any nonzero size means unexpected trailing data and is rejected. This is the terminal integrity gate on the block stream.

The shared-library tail. First loop: at the second PT_LOAD it captures load_off, seeks to old_data_off, and reads old_data_len bytes into ibuf (then rewrites them to the output). Second loop: at the PT_DYNAMIC segment it forms a pointer 'ibuf + (dyn_off - load_off)' and walks dyn_len bytes of Elf64_Dyn entries looking for DT_INIT to patch old_dtinit into the output. old_data_len, dyn_off, dyn_len and load_off are all attacker-controlled 64-bit phdr fields; the pointer arithmetic (dyn_off - load_off) and the loop bound dyn_len are used to index ibuf with no check against ibuf's capacity.

Final bookkeeping: record the accumulated compressed/uncompressed totals, require that the total output equals the claimed orig_file_size, and verify the running Adler-32 checksums against the stored header values. These end-of-run checks catch inconsistent streams, but they run only after all the parsing/copying above has already executed, so they do not protect the earlier memory operations.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: ibuf was allocated with capacity blocksize+OVERHEAD (line 39), a bound derived only from p_blocksize. Here the shlib path reads 'overlay_offset + sizeof(hbuf) + szb_info + ph.c_len' bytes into ibuf. overlay_offset is the (attacker-influenced) file position of the overlay and is completely unrelated to blocksize; nothing constrains this sum to <= ibuf.getSize(). If overlay_offset (plus the constants and ph.c_len) exceeds blocksize+OVERHEAD, readx writes past the end of the heap buffer — a remote heap buffer overflow (CWE-787). The implementation must clamp the read length to ibuf.getSize() or reject overlay_offset that exceeds the allocation.

old_data_len was set at line 96 from get_te64(&phdr->p_filesz) — a fully attacker-controlled 64-bit program-header field, captured while walking u_phnum entries. It is never checked against ibuf's capacity (blocksize+OVERHEAD) before this readx copies old_data_len bytes into ibuf. A crafted second PT_LOAD with a large p_filesz overflows the heap buffer (CWE-787); the subsequent fo->rewrite(ibuf, old_data_len) then also reads old_data_len bytes back out of ibuf (CWE-125). The invariant is old_data_len <= ibuf.getSize().

dyn_off (from p_offset) and load_off (from the second PT_LOAD's p_offset) are both attacker-controlled. The base pointer is ibuf + (dyn_off - load_off): if dyn_off < load_off the unsigned subtraction wraps to a huge value, pointing far outside ibuf; even without wrap, a large (dyn_off - load_off) points past the buffer. The loop then reads Elf64_Dyn entries for dyn_len bytes (dyn_len is p_filesz, attacker-controlled) with no bound against ibuf's size, dereferencing dyn->d_tag out of bounds (CWE-125), and can drive an out-of-bounds output rewrite (CWE-787). The invariant: dyn_off >= load_off and (dyn_off - load_off) + dyn_len <= ibuf.getSize().

u_phnum comes from the decompressed ehdr->e_phnum (line 67) and can be up to 65535, but u.buf is only MAX_ELF_HDR = 1024 bytes and holds at most ~17 Elf64_Phdr entries after the 64-byte Ehdr. The b_info guard (line 45) bounds the number of *decompressed bytes* to sizeof(u), but nothing bounds e_phnum against that byte count. This loop (and the identical u_phnum-bounded walks at lines 93, 107, 120, 167, 198, 213) indexes phdr[j] past the end of the 1024-byte stack union, an out-of-bounds read of stack memory (CWE-125) whose values then steer offsets/sizes handed to unpackExtent. The implementation must reject u_phnum whose phdr table would exceed the decompressed header bytes / u.buf capacity.

This unsigned 64-bit subtraction sets the amount to decompress for the tail of the first PT_LOAD. p_filesz is attacker-controlled; if it is smaller than overlay_offset the result wraps to a value near 2^64, which is then passed to unpackExtent(ph.u_len, ...) as the uncompressed length to produce. That is the classic underflow-to-huge-size primitive (CWE-191) leading to over-long output/decompression. The code must verify p_filesz >= overlay_offset (reject when too short) before subtracting.

Both the source pointer (ibuf + ph.u_len) and the length (overlay_offset - ph.u_len) depend on attacker-controlled values. If ph.u_len > overlay_offset the length underflows to a huge unsigned value (CWE-191), and ibuf + ph.u_len can already point past the just-read region, so the write copies far more than ibuf holds — an out-of-bounds read feeding the output (CWE-125). The invariant is ph.u_len <= overlay_offset and ph.u_len within the bytes actually read into ibuf at line 85.

phdr is set to the class member phdri and immediately dereferenced (phdr->p_type) inside a c_phnum-bounded loop, with no NULL guard here. If the container-parse path left phdri NULL (or if c_phnum overstates the phdri allocation), this dereferences a NULL/undersized pointer — a NULL-pointer dereference / out-of-bounds read (CWE-476 / CWE-125). Note the local cursor 'phdr' was initialised to 0 at line 37, underlining that a NULL phdr is a reachable state in this function; the implementation must guarantee phdri is non-NULL and holds c_phnum valid entries before this walk.

lsize is from linfo.l_lsize (attacker-controlled, 16-bit). sizeof(d_info) is 24. 'lsize - sizeof(d_info)' is evaluated in unsigned/size_t arithmetic, so any lsize < 24 wraps to a near-SIZE_MAX seek delta (CWE-191). This corrupts the input file position rather than memory directly, but a wild seek can desynchronise all subsequent readx calls (and, combined with the size fields above, contribute to the memory issues). The implementation should validate lsize >= sizeof(d_info).

summary: This UPX PackLinuxElf64::unpack routine parses a fully attacker-controlled packed ELF64 and reconstructs it, but trusts several attacker-supplied 64-bit program-header sizes/offsets and the decompressed e_phnum count without bounding them against the fixed 1024-byte stack union u.buf or the blocksize+OVERHEAD heap buffer ibuf. The result is a cluster of memory-safety hazards: heap overflows via readx lengths derived from overlay_offset and p_filesz (lines 85, 202), out-of-bounds reads from unbounded u_phnum phdr walks and from the (dyn_off - load_off) pointer arithmetic (lines 160-165, 217-219), and unsigned underflows in size computations (lines 88, 109, 156). Overall risk is high for a tool routinely run on untrusted binaries.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Structural sanity check that aborts; imposes a constraint rather than consuming a length into a buffer.
Attacker-controlled 16-bit count that bounds the phdri walk at line 140 with no capacity check.
Selects a header size (8 or sizeof(b_info)); both are small fixed reads into fixed structs, no overflow surface.
Attacker-controlled loader size used in the underflowing seek at line 156.
Used only in comparison guards (lines 27, 238); not used as a copy length.
Bounded by the p_info guard and mem_size_valid before sizing ibuf; the guard is the mitigation.
This is a validation gate, the mitigation for blocksize; no contract of its own violated.
Fixed 1024-byte destination indexed by attacker-controlled u_phnum in multiple phdr walks.
phdr initialised NULL; the phdr=phdri walk at line 138 can dereference a NULL/undersized pointer (CWE-476).
Heap buffer whose capacity is exceeded by the readx at lines 85 and 202.
Both are bounded by the b_info guard (lines 44-46) and the decompression re-check (line 50) before use as decompression sizes.
Validation gate bounding ph.u_len<=sizeof(u) and ph.c_len<=file_size; it is the mitigation, but it does NOT bound u_phnum.
Correctly bounds the decompression byte count against ibuf/u.buf; the residual gap is the phdr count, tracked separately.
Both operands are attacker-influenced, so it is an internal-consistency check with no buffer contract.
Attacker-controlled 16-bit count driving every phdr walk over u.buf and ibuf with no capacity bound.
Boolean classifier over container phdrs; selects a code path but carries no direct buffer contract itself.
Read length derived from overlay_offset, unrelated to ibuf capacity -> heap overflow write (CWE-787).
Unsigned underflow of the write length when ph.u_len > overlay_offset (CWE-191).
old_data_len is used unbounded as the readx length into ibuf at line 202.
Unsigned 64-bit underflow to a huge decompress size (CWE-191).
Same unbounded-u_phnum OOB read root cause as line 160-165; folded into the u_phnum finding to avoid duplicate findings.
Dereferences member phdri without NULL guard (CWE-476).
Index is 2 or 3 into a 6-element local array; in bounds.
Unsigned underflow of the seek delta when lsize < 24 (CWE-191); file-position corruption.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: gap loops over u.buf, u_phnum bound (lines 160-178)
reviewed_anchors.reason: phdr[j] indexes the 1024-byte u.buf with attacker-controlled u_phnum -> OOB read (CWE-125).
Terminal integrity gate comparing sz_cpr to the magic; a check, not a buffer op.
Unbounded old_data_len copied into/out of ibuf -> heap overflow (CWE-787).
Pointer underflow plus unbounded dyn_len walk of ibuf -> OOB read/write (CWE-125/191).
Writes a fixed 8 bytes to the output file at an attacker-chosen offset; output file is trusted and offset is a file position, not a memory index.
Post-hoc integrity checks; run after all copies, so they gate acceptance but not the earlier memory ops.

findings.title: Heap overflow: readx length derived from overlay_offset exceeds ibuf capacity
findings.invariant: The number of bytes read into ibuf must never exceed ibuf.getSize() (== blocksize + OVERHEAD).
findings.failure_mode: ibuf is sized from p_blocksize alone, but the read length adds overlay_offset (the attacker-controlled overlay file position) plus ph.c_len. A packed shared library with a large overlay_offset/ph.c_len makes the sum exceed blocksize+OVERHEAD, so readx writes past the heap allocation, corrupting adjacent heap memory and enabling remote code execution when upx unpacks the file.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether readx clamps to ibuf.getSize() or whether overlay_offset+sizeof(hbuf)+szb_info+ph.c_len is validated <= ibuf.getSize() before this call.

findings.title: Heap overflow: attacker-controlled old_data_len (p_filesz) copied into ibuf
findings.invariant: old_data_len (from the second PT_LOAD's p_filesz) must be <= ibuf.getSize() before being used as a read/rewrite length.
findings.failure_mode: old_data_len is set at line 96 from get_te64(&phdr->p_filesz), a 64-bit attacker-controlled program-header field, and is never checked against ibuf's blocksize+OVERHEAD capacity. A crafted second PT_LOAD with a large p_filesz overflows ibuf here (and the following fo->rewrite(ibuf, old_data_len) over-reads it), giving heap corruption / OOB read.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify old_data_len is bounded by ibuf.getSize() (and by the actual file remainder) before the readx and the subsequent rewrite.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read: unbounded u_phnum drives phdr walks over the fixed 1024-byte u.buf
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (count field vs fixed buffer)
findings.invariant: u_phnum must be bounded so that sizeof(Elf64_Ehdr) + u_phnum*sizeof(Elf64_Phdr) <= MAX_ELF_HDR (1024) / the number of decompressed header bytes.
findings.failure_mode: u_phnum = get_te16(&ehdr->e_phnum) can be up to 65535, but u.buf holds only ~17 Elf64_Phdr entries. The b_info guard bounds the decompressed byte count, not the phdr count, so every u_phnum-bounded walk (lines 93, 107, 120, 160-168, 198, 213) reads Elf64_Phdr entries past the end of the 1024-byte stack union, leaking adjacent stack memory into offsets/sizes that then steer unpackExtent seeks and lengths.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether e_phnum is validated against (ph.u_len - sizeof(Ehdr))/sizeof(Phdr) or against MAX_ELF_HDR before these loops.

findings.title: OOB read/write: (dyn_off - load_off) pointer underflow and unbounded dyn_len walk of ibuf
findings.invariant: dyn_off >= load_off and (dyn_off - load_off) + dyn_len <= ibuf.getSize(); dyn_len must be a valid multiple/size within ibuf.
findings.failure_mode: dyn_off, load_off and dyn_len are all attacker-controlled 64-bit phdr fields. If dyn_off < load_off the unsigned subtraction wraps huge and the base pointer lands far outside ibuf; the loop then dereferences dyn->d_tag for dyn_len bytes with no bound against ibuf's size, causing an out-of-bounds read (and, on a DT_INIT match, an attacker-directed output rewrite). Any dyn_off/dyn_len past the buffer over-reads heap memory.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the code checks dyn_off >= load_off and that (dyn_off - load_off) + dyn_len stays within ibuf before dereferencing dyn.

findings.title: Unsigned underflow: p_filesz - overlay_offset yields a huge decompress size
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow (unsigned subtraction) feeding a size
findings.invariant: p_filesz of the first PT_LOAD must be >= overlay_offset before the subtraction; reject the file otherwise.
findings.failure_mode: p_filesz is attacker-controlled. If it is smaller than overlay_offset, the 64-bit unsigned subtraction wraps to a value near 2^64, which is passed to unpackExtent as ph.u_len (the amount to produce), driving an over-long decompression/output — a classic underflow-to-huge-size primitive.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify a 'reject if p_filesz < overlay_offset' guard exists before this subtraction.

findings.title: Unsigned underflow: overlay_offset - ph.u_len as an oversized write length
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow (unsigned subtraction) feeding a length
findings.invariant: ph.u_len <= overlay_offset, and ibuf must actually contain overlay_offset bytes read at line 85.
findings.failure_mode: If ph.u_len > overlay_offset the length 'overlay_offset - ph.u_len' wraps to a huge unsigned value and the source pointer ibuf+ph.u_len may already point past the read region, so fo->write copies far beyond ibuf's contents — an out-of-bounds read into the output. Reachable only when fo is non-NULL.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm ph.u_len is validated <= overlay_offset and within the bytes read into ibuf before this write.

findings.title: Possible NULL/undersized pointer dereference of phdri in the load_va scan
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — depends on how phdri is populated for the container
findings.invariant: phdri must be non-NULL and point to at least c_phnum valid Elf64_Phdr entries before this walk.
findings.failure_mode: phdr is assigned the class member phdri and dereferenced (phdr->p_type) inside a c_phnum-bounded loop with no local NULL/size check. If the container-parse path left phdri NULL (the local phdr was even initialised to 0 at line 37) or if the attacker-controlled c_phnum overstates the phdri allocation, this reads through a NULL/undersized pointer, crashing or reading out of bounds.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm phdri is guaranteed non-NULL with c_phnum valid entries at this point (i.e. the container phdr table was successfully parsed and bounded).

findings.title: Unsigned underflow of seek delta when lsize < sizeof(d_info)
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow (unsigned subtraction) in file positioning
findings.invariant: lsize (linfo.l_lsize) must be >= sizeof(d_info) (24) before computing the skip delta.
findings.failure_mode: lsize is an attacker-controlled 16-bit field. 'lsize - sizeof(d_info)' is computed in unsigned/size_t arithmetic, so lsize < 24 wraps to a near-SIZE_MAX seek offset, desynchronising the input file position for all subsequent readx calls. This is a robustness/position-corruption issue rather than a direct memory-safety break.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify lsize is validated >= sizeof(d_info) before this seek.

audit_checklist: Bound e_phnum/u_phnum against (decompressed header bytes - sizeof(Ehdr))/sizeof(Phdr) and MAX_ELF_HDR before every phdr walk over u.buf and ibuf.
Clamp the line-85 readx length (overlay_offset + sizeof(hbuf) + szb_info + ph.c_len) to ibuf.getSize(), or reject overlay_offset that would exceed the allocation.
Validate old_data_len (p_filesz of the 2nd PT_LOAD) <= ibuf.getSize() and <= remaining file bytes before readx/rewrite at lines 202/207.
Guard the DT_INIT loop: require dyn_off >= load_off and (dyn_off - load_off) + dyn_len <= ibuf.getSize() before forming the dyn pointer (lines 217-219).
Add 'reject if too short before subtracting' guards for p_filesz - overlay_offset (line 109), overlay_offset - ph.u_len (line 88), and lsize - sizeof(d_info) (line 156).
Confirm phdri is non-NULL and holds c_phnum valid entries before the load_va scan at line 138.
Check that filesz/offset truncation from 64-bit to unsigned in the main-exec loop (lines 122-123) cannot drop high bits to bypass later bounds.
Ensure the final orig_file_size / Adler-32 checks (lines 238-243) are not the only defense — they run after all copies have executed.
